questions.text: In the story 'His First Flight', why was the young seagull afraid to fly?
options: He thought his wings would never support him
His parents didn't want him to
questions.explanation: The young seagull lacked confidence in his own physical abilities despite having wings.

questions.text: How many siblings did the young seagull have?
questions.explanation: He had two brothers and one sister who had already learned to fly.

questions.text: For how long had the young seagull been alone on the ledge?
questions.explanation: His family had flown away a day before, leaving him without food.

questions.text: What was the young seagull's mother doing when he looked at her from the ledge?
options: Tearing at a piece of fish
questions.explanation: She was standing on a plateau, sharpening her beak on a rock after tearing some fish.

questions.text: What did the young seagull pretend to do to get his family's attention?
options: Falling asleep while standing on one leg
questions.explanation: He stood on the edge of the ledge on one leg with the other hidden to evoke sympathy.

questions.text: The sight of ______ maddened the young seagull.
questions.explanation: He was extremely hungry, and seeing his mother with fish made his hunger unbearable.

questions.text: What compelled the young seagull to finally dive from the ledge?
questions.explanation: He dove at the fish in his mother's beak out of sheer desperation and hunger.

questions.text: What was the young seagull's first sensation as he fell into space?
questions.explanation: Initially, he was gripped by fear and his heart stood still as he fell.

questions.text: Where did the young seagull land after his first flight?
questions.explanation: He landed on the surface of the water, which he initially thought was solid green floor.

questions.text: What did the young seagull's family offer him as a reward after his flight?
questions.explanation: They celebrated his success by feeding him pieces of dog-fish.

questions.text: The story 'His First Flight' is a metaphor for:
questions.explanation: It emphasizes that one must take a leap of faith to discover their inner potential.

questions.text: In 'The Black Aeroplane', what was the pilot's destination?
questions.explanation: The pilot was flying from France back to his home in England.

questions.text: What plane was the pilot flying?
questions.explanation: The pilot was flying an old Dakota airplane.

questions.text: Why was the pilot happy at the start of the journey?
options: He was looking forward to a holiday and breakfast with family
questions.explanation: The moon was up, the stars were shining, and he was dreaming of his holiday.

questions.text: When did the pilot see the storm clouds?
options: When he was 150 km past Paris
questions.explanation: Suddenly, huge black mountains of clouds appeared in front of him.

questions.text: Why didn't the pilot fly around the storm clouds?
options: He wanted to test his skills
questions.explanation: He couldn't fly up and over them or around them to the north or south because of low fuel.

questions.text: What happened to the instruments inside the storm?
options: The compass was turning round and round and the radio was dead
questions.explanation: The storm caused a total failure of the navigation and communication systems.

questions.text: What did the pilot see in the black clouds that gave him hope?
questions.explanation: A mysterious black plane appeared without lights on its wings.

questions.text: How did the mysterious pilot help the narrator?
options: By waving and leading him through the clouds
questions.explanation: The other pilot turned his plane slowly to the north so that the narrator could follow him easily.

questions.text: After landing, what did the woman in the control center tell the pilot?
options: The other pilot was waiting for him
There was no other plane in the sky except his
He was in the wrong city
questions.explanation: She looked at him strangely and said no other planes were flying in the storm.

questions.text: The theme of 'The Black Aeroplane' is:
options: The mystery of the unknown and supernatural help
A story of a plane crash
questions.explanation: It explores an unexplained experience where the pilot is saved by a 'ghost' plane.

questions.text: The young seagull's father and mother were ______ him to take his first flight.
questions.explanation: They tried to force him to fly by threatening to let him starve on the ledge.

questions.text: Who is the author of 'His First Flight'?
questions.explanation: The Irish novelist and short story writer Liam O'Flaherty wrote this story.

questions.text: Who is the author of 'The Black Aeroplane'?
questions.explanation: Frederick Forsyth is a famous English novelist known for thrillers.

questions.text: In 'His First Flight', what does 'upbraiding' mean?
questions.explanation: His parents were calling to him shrilly, upbraiding (scolding) him for his cowardice.

questions.text: What was the young seagull's sister doing while he was on the ledge?
questions.explanation: All his siblings had already mastered the art of flying and catching prey.

questions.text: Why did the narrator think 'Everything was going well' at the start of 'The Black Aeroplane'?
options: The weather was clear and moon was up
questions.explanation: Initial conditions were perfect, making the sudden storm even more shocking.

questions.text: What was the risk the pilot took in 'The Black Aeroplane'?
questions.explanation: He decided to go straight into the storm because he wanted to get home for breakfast.

questions.text: The narrator says, 'I was happy to be alone high up above the sleeping countryside.' Which countryside is he referring to?
questions.explanation: He was flying over France towards England.

questions.text: The word 'precipice' in 'His First Flight' refers to:
options: A very steep rock face or cliff
questions.explanation: The young seagull was standing on the edge of a high cliff (precipice).

questions.text: How did the young seagull feel when his belly touched the green sea?
questions.explanation: After the initial panic, he found that he could float on the water without effort.

questions.text: In 'The Black Aeroplane', what did the pilot do first after landing?
options: Went to the control tower to ask about the other pilot
questions.explanation: He was desperate to thank the person who saved his life.

questions.text: What does the 'black aeroplane' symbolize?
questions.explanation: It represents an external manifestation of the pilot's own will to survive or a divine intervention.

questions.text: The seagull's 'first flight' was a success because of his ______.
questions.explanation: His mother tempted him with food, forcing him to fly to reach it.

questions.text: What was the name of the first city the pilot passed in 'The Black Aeroplane'?
questions.explanation: He called Paris Control when he was on his way.

questions.text: The Young Seagull was 'maddened by hunger'. This shows that:
options: Necessity is the mother of invention/action
questions.explanation: Hunger pushed him to overcome a fear that logic and threats could not.

questions.text: Why did the pilot's compass stop working?
options: Because of the magnetic interference in the storm
questions.explanation: The storm was so intense that it disabled his electrical and mechanical equipment.

questions.text: How did the young seagull feel when he started flying?
options: He felt a bit dizzy, then confident
questions.explanation: After the first minute, his wings spread and he realized he was flying.

questions.text: The pilot was 'frightened' again in the storm when he realized:
options: He only had fuel for 5 or 10 minutes more
questions.explanation: Low fuel meant he had a very limited window to land safely.

questions.text: Which of these words best describes the young seagull's family?
options: Strict but encouraging in their own way
questions.explanation: They used 'tough love' to ensure their child learned a vital life skill.
